texto: For a kid in crisis, there is no "make it happen," only "survive today." Who am I to have the cojones to think my "critical questions" are the most important thing in this kid's life? I think of the times I was in crisis and failed to pay attention to the manila folders on my desk as an adult.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Dawn Casey-Rowe que provoca passagem do tempo e prioridades. Ao questionar “For a kid in crisis, there is no "make it happen," only "survive today." Who am I to have the coj…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, crisis, kids, com foco em kids e survival.

frase_original: For a kid in crisis, there is no "make it happen," only "survive today." Who am I to have the cojones to think my "critical questions" are the most important thing in this kid's life? I think of the times I was in crisis and failed to pay attention to the manila folders on my desk as an adult.

texto: One day, the lotus spoke again. "You remember me? The flower that grows through the mud?" I did. I said as much. "Have you ever considered my significance? I'm everywhere - art, religion, nature.... Have you ever wondered why?" ... It spoke, "Nothing touches me. I radiate beauty. You can do the same." "How?" I asked. "Easy," it said. "I grow in a pond. I take the water and nutrients I need to grow, and let the rest sink to the bottom. What's in mud, anyway? Water, nutrients, life and a little bit of sludge. Let the sludge go like I do. Then stand tall above the leaves.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Dawn Casey-Rowe: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de sentido da existência e experiência vivida. O trecho “One day, the lotus spoke again. "You remember me? The flower that grows through the mud?" I did. …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que fe, beauty, vida pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de vida e lotus.
